comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around physical and digital implementations of Conway's Game of Life, with users sharing experiences, ideas for cost reduction, and curiosity about large-scale or alternative versions. Some comments highlight existing installations and potential creative uses, while others express enthusiasm or skepticism about the project's physicality.

comments.author: eps
comments.text: I saw one in a computer museum in Switzerland. It was a much larger field, it was just large orange LEDs (or were they tubes?), but it also cycled between a dozen of different cell automata games. Something about being able to see individual "pixels" made it really mesmerizing.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a credible firsthand account of a physical installation of cellular automata, adding valuable context to the discussion.

comments.author: PetitPrince
comments.text: My Alma matter has a jumbo version of this, in which the game if life is one of several available mode https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BioWall
comments.analysis: The mention of a specific installation with a reference link adds factual weight and enriches the conversation with a real-world example.

comments.author: slow_typist
comments.text:
Très cool.

A grid of capacitive touch sensors could be printed directly on the pcb, bringing down costs by a degree of magnitude. Real switches are much more satisfying though.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a practical suggestion for cost reduction while acknowledging user experience considerations, showing balanced insight.

comments.author: self_awareness
comments.text: That's not a "physical" version of game of life -- that's a digital version, like every version, but with bigger pixels.
comments.analysis: This comment critically distinguishes between physical and digital implementations, clarifying a common misconception in the discussion.

comments.author: mastermedo
comments.text: A thousand bucks for 17x17 touchscreen. Add a painting frame, hang it on the wall, and you made yourself amazing art for cheap.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a realistic cost estimate and a creative use-case, contributing practical perspective to the conversation.

comments.author: CJefferson
comments.text: I've always wanted something like this board, buttons which can light up (preferably a few colours), to use to make games. Anyone ever found such a board which is hackable / programmable?
comments.analysis: This comment expresses genuine interest and seeks community input, fostering constructive engagement.

comments.author: vunderba
comments.text:
Nice. A friend of mine just picked up a Linnstrument, and I’m very tempted to create a Conway’s Game of Life-based musical visualization for it.

https://www.rogerlinndesign.com/linnstrument
comments.analysis: This comment creatively links the Game of Life to musical visualization, showing innovative thinking though not directly about the physical implementation.

comments.author: shawoodle65
comments.text: surprised this isn't talked about more
comments.analysis: This brief comment expresses surprise but lacks substantive content or evidence.

comments.author: exolab
comments.text:
> I figured out what would be a reasonable amount to spend on the project and then multiplied that by 10.

I like the way you think.
comments.analysis: This comment is a lighthearted reaction with no factual contribution to the discussion.

comments.author: fwipsy
comments.text: I don't want to build this or pay for it, but I really want to mess with it for an hour.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses personal interest but adds no analytical or factual value.
